metadata.description: The multi-cluster management plane (ACM hub / HCP management cluster / Rancher server / hosted fleet manager). Adopts Cluster API's management-cluster vocabulary and OCM's hub/ManagedCluster model; relationship semantics per ADR-043 (typed target carries meaning; self-managed hubs hold no ordering edge into their host chain).

context.purpose: The multi-cluster management plane: the thing that provisions, imports, and lifecycle-manages a fleet of clusters.
context.plain_description: A Hub is whatever sits above your clusters and manages them as a fleet — an ACM hub, a HyperShift/HCP management cluster, a Rancher server, or a hosted fleet manager. It may itself run on a cluster (including, in the self-managed pattern, the very cluster it manages) or be standalone software. Model the hub as its own entity; whether it lives on a cluster is just an edge.
use_when: you need to record which management plane provisioned or now manages a cluster
a cluster's lifecycle operations flow through a fleet manager and shutdown/startup order must respect it
you need the management plane's own sovereignty position (which jurisdiction governs the manager, distinct from its spokes)
not_for: the cluster a hub happens to run on — that host is a plain Compute.Cluster, and hub-ness on it is a derived role marker, never authored
single-cluster platform services (GitOps controllers, ingress operators) — those are Software.Service on the cluster
a peer DCM instance in federation — that is the federate capability on the provider contract, not a Hub
works_with: Compute.Cluster — spokes point at the hub (contained_by when hub-provisioned/hosted-control-plane; depends_on soft when imported), and a hosted hub points contained_by at its own host cluster
Facility.Location — where the hub's control plane actually runs, for the sovereignty question
Security.CredentialRef — the fleet-management credentials the hub holds are references, never inline

spec.description: Portable hub surface. Deliberately thin: what a hub IS to the model (identity, reachability, fleet role) — hub-product specifics (ACM policy sets, Rancher projects, HCP sizing knobs) are Provider-Class SharedDataElements (ADR-038), documented exclusions per rule 36(a). Relationship semantics follow ADR-043: meaning lives in the typed target, ordering in edge_type; the self-managed hub holds no ordering edge into its own host chain (references only — the CYCLE gate enforces it).

api_endpoint.description: The hub's management API endpoint (URL). The address spokes and operators talk to.

hosted_control_planes.description: Whether this hub hosts spoke control planes inside itself (HyperShift/HCP pattern). Hosted spokes are contained_by this hub; imported spokes are depends_on (soft).

hub_ready.description: The management plane is up and serving its API — the realize-time fact a spoke-provisioning request binds on.

api_endpoint.description: Realized management API endpoint (may differ from intent when the provider assigns it).

managed_cluster_count.description: Fleet rollup at last reconciliation: how many clusters point at this hub (contained_by + depends_on inbound). A reading of the graph, published for cheap binding.

relationships.description: The host cluster, when the hub runs on one (ACM hub, HCP management cluster). Standalone hubs (Rancher server, hosted fleet managers) omit it. In the self-managed pattern the host is also a spoke: that reflexive management edge is expressed as references on the spoke side, never as an ordering edge back into this host chain (ADR-043; CYCLE-gate enforced).

adopts.role: the industry vocabulary for exactly this split: the management cluster provisions and manages workload clusters; Platform.Hub is the management-cluster identity, Compute.Cluster edges are the workload side
ACM's upstream hub-spoke model: the hub cluster and its ManagedCluster resources — the imported-spoke (depends_on soft) semantics mirror OCM's non-destructive import/detach
